1: Psalm 12's superscription mentions the "Sheminith" (šĕmînît) — possibly an eight-stringed instrument, a musical register (eighth = bass), or a liturgical category. The same direction appears in Ps 6. The psalm is a lament over total collapse of faithful speech in society. The hyperbole "the faithful have vanished from among humanity" is characteristic of the lament genre's extreme formulation (cf. Elijah's "I alone am left," 1 Kgs 19:10) — not a census claim but a rhetorical expression of moral isolation. The crisis of trustworthy speech was perennial in ancient courts and communities.
2: "Everyone lies to his neighbor; they speak with flattering lips and a divided heart (lēb wālēb)" — lēb wālēb (heart and heart = divided heart) is the idiom for duplicity: one public heart, one hidden heart. To have two hearts violated the fundamental social bond of covenant community. In ancient Israelite ethics, integrity meant the alignment of speech and heart (cf. Ps 15:2 — "speaks truth from the heart"). Šāwĕʾ (falsehood, emptiness) is paired with smooth speech — the combination of empty words and manipulative delivery.
3: "May the LORD cut off all flattering lips" — the petition against siptê ḥălāqôt (smooth/flattering lips) and lāšôn mĕdabberet gĕdōlôt (tongue that speaks great things, boastfully). The contrast throughout the psalm is between two kinds of speech: human speech (manipulative, boastful, self-serving) and divine speech (pure, reliable — v. 6). In ANE thought, words carried power — false words damaged the social fabric by corrupting the medium through which the community sustained trust and covenant loyalty.
4: "With our words we will prevail; our lips are our own — who can lord it over us?" — the claim of autonomous speech: no accountability to God or community. In ancient culture, one's word was one's bond, and to renege on it was to damage one's social honor (kābôd). The arrogance here is cosmological: to claim autonomy of speech is to deny that language is given by and accountable to the Creator. "Who can lord it over us?" denies any transcendent authority — a posture the psalm regards as both morally and theologically self-deceptive.
5: "'Because the poor are oppressed and the needy groan, I will now arise,' says the LORD" — a direct divine oracle (nĕʾum yhwh) embedded within the lament, a technique more common in prophetic literature than the Psalms (cf. Isa 33:10 — "now I will arise"). The direct divine response to the poor's cry mirrors the Exodus pattern: God hears the groaning (nĕʾāqāh) of the oppressed and acts (Exod 2:24; 6:5). "I will set them in the safety for which they long (yāpîaḥ lô)" — the safety is precisely the covenantal protection the corrupt community has denied them.
6: "The words of the LORD are pure words, like silver refined in a furnace in the ground, purified seven times" — the great contrast. Human speech is corrupt (vv. 2-4); divine speech is maximally pure. The metallurgical image is precise: silver refined in a clay furnace (ʿălîl lāʾāreṣ), heated repeatedly to remove all dross. Seven is the number of completeness — sevenfold refining = absolute purity. This is the standard by which all other speech is measured. Second Temple Judaism applied this to Torah: God's word is perfectly reliable (cf. Ps 19:7-10).
7: "You, O LORD, will keep them; you will protect us from this generation (haddôr hazzeh) forever" — the pronouns shift ambiguously (them/us) but the meaning is clear: God preserves both his words and those who trust in them against the corrupt generation. "This generation" (haddôr hazzeh) is a recurring moral category in Scripture — not a biological cohort but a spiritually defined community characterized by faithlessness (cf. Deut 1:35; Ps 78:8; Matt 12:39). The righteous are embedded in history alongside the corrupt and require divine protection.
8: "The wicked parade on every side when what is vile is honored among humanity" — the closing observation rather than resolution. When the honor-system is corrupted — when the dishonorable gain honor — the wicked multiply unchecked. Honor and shame were the primary social regulators in ancient Mediterranean culture; when the system inverts (vileness honored rather than shamed), the mechanisms of social accountability collapse entirely. The psalm ends with this ongoing reality, having placed its hope entirely in the divine speech of vv. 5-7 rather than in social reform.
